ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for Aloo Kulcha; mapped to flatbread food product as the closest defensible ancestor class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bread and similar products — leavened flatbread with filling, wheat-based, tandoor-baked
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Aloo Kulcha; A000E (bread and similar products) used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Aloo Kulcha or equivalent stuffed Indian flatbread.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of Aloo Kulcha weighs approximately 180 g, as commonly served at North Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Indian Council of Medical Research, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN does not publish a direct entry for Aloo Kulcha as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for maida-based leavened bread and boiled potato filling components, weighted by typical recipe proportions (approximately 65% dough, 35% potato filling). The input record values (energy 233 kcal, protein 5.56 g, fat 10.0 g, carbohydrate 30.56 g per 100 g) are consistent with this composition and are retained with medium confidence. Additional micronutrient values (calcium, iron, vitamin C) are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for wheat flour and potato.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson, Foster-Powell, Brand-Miller
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Aloo Kulcha in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 67 is estimated from the closest analogous food: white wheat flatbread / naan (GI ~67–70, University of Sydney GI Database). The potato filling raises the effective GI slightly; however, the dough component dominates by weight (~65%), so GI 67 is used as a conservative analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (67 × 30.56 × 1.80) / 100 ≈ 36.5 g per 180 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 361 mg per 100 g; one serving (180 g) delivers approximately 650 mg sodium, representing 28–43% of the recommended daily limit of 1500–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Aloo Kulcha to half a piece per meal, request reduced salt in both dough and filling, and avoid additional ghee or butter brushing to minimise combined sodium and saturated fat load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load of 36.5 per serving driven by refined wheat flour (maida) dough and high-GI potato filling; dietary fibre only 2.22 g per 100 g provides insufficient glycaemic buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict Aloo Kulcha to half a piece, pair it with a high-protein accompaniment such as dal or paneer, and request a whole-wheat flour version to reduce the glycaemic load and improve postprandial blood glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.44 g per 100 g (approximately 8 g per serving) from ghee and butter brushing, combined with trans fat 0.28 g per 100 g and high sodium, elevates cardiovascular risk at regular consumption.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Aloo Kulcha to occasional consumption, request minimal or no ghee or butter brushing, and choose whole-wheat flour versions to reduce saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium intake per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Potato and green chilli are nightshade vegetables containing solanine, which may aggravate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals; refined wheat flour drives pro-inflammatory glycaemic load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshades should avoid Aloo Kulcha; if consumed, request omission of chilli, substitute ghee with olive oil, and choose a whole-wheat flour version to reduce inflammatory glycaemic load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Kulcha is a traditional North Indian leavened flatbread originating from the Punjab region of India. It is made from refined wheat flour (maida) dough that is leavened with yogurt and baking powder, then stuffed with a spiced potato filling typically containing mashed potato, onion, green chilli, fresh coriander, cumin, and salt. The stuffed dough is sealed, flattened, and baked in a tandoor (clay oven) at high heat until golden brown and slightly charred on the surface, then brushed generously with butter or ghee. The result is a soft, fluffy, slightly crisp flatbread with a savoury, mildly spiced potato interior. Aloo Kulcha is commonly served with chole (spiced chickpea curry), raita, or pickle, and is a popular street food and restaurant dish across North India and in Indian diaspora communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, one piece (approximately 180 g) provides around 420 kcal, 10 g protein, 18 g fat, and 55 g carbohydrate. The high refined carbohydrate content, moderate-to-high sodium (approximately 650 mg per serving), and saturated fat from ghee make it a food of concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The potato and chilli filling contains nightshade compounds that may be relevant for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Whole-wheat flour versions and reduced-ghee preparations are healthier alternatives.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component data for maida-based bread and boiled potato filling, weighted by typical recipe proportions. No single approved-source entry for Aloo Kulcha as a composite dish exists in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore. GI is an analogous estimate from white wheat flatbread data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adaptation basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated), B-vitamin profile (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, folate), fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E), mineral fractions (potassium, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium), and moisture data for Aloo Kulcha as a composite dish are not individually reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific preparation as of 2024. These fields are set to -1 to indicate data unavailability rather than a true zero value. All core macros (protein_g 5.56, fat_g 10.0, carbohydrate_g 30.56) are non-zero and consistent with the ICMR-NIN adapted composition for a maida-and-potato stuffed flatbread. trans_fat_g 0.28 is retained from the input record; this is plausible given ghee and commercial butter use in preparation. Cultural note: Aloo Kulcha is widely served in Singapore at North Indian restaurants such as Bawarchi North Indian Cuisine; the dish is classified as halal and vegetarian in this context. The primary_cuisine field in the input referred to a specific restaurant name and has been normalised to North Indian cuisine.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed from input JSON with ICMR-NIN adapted nutrient values and University of Sydney GI analogous estimate.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'; added -1 field documentation to notes field.

Iteration 3: Expanded notes field to explicitly document the missing-data basis for every -1 sentinel value in per_100g, resolving g3_numeric_hygiene failure. Each -1 field is now individually named with the reason (data not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this composite dish preparation as of 2024).
